file_name: SightLife Surgical, Inc. - STRATEGIC SALES _ MARKETING AGREEMENT.txt

perturbation.original_text:
11. Indemnification and Insurance.
11.1. Each party shall indemnify and hold harmless the other party, and its directors, officers, employees and agents, from and against all losses, liabilities, damages and expenses, including reasonable attorneys' fees and costs (collectively, "Liabilities"), resulting from any claims, demands, actions or other proceedings by any third party to the extent resulting from the breach of any representation, warranty or covenant by the breaching party under this Agreement.
11.2. Insurance. Each party shall maintain insurance, including comprehensive or commercial general liability and products liability insurance (contractual liability included), with respect to its activities under this Agreement in such amounts and with such limits as reasonable and customary in the industry, but with limits not less than the following: (a) each occurrence, one million dollars ($1,000,000); (b) products/completed operations aggregate, five million dollars ($5,000,000); (c) personal and advertising injury, one million dollars ($1,000,000); and (d) general aggregate (commercial form only), five million dollars ($5,000,000). Each Party shall maintain such insurance for so long as it continues its activities under this Agreement, and thereafter for so long as it customarily maintains insurance for itself covering similar activities.
perturbation.changed_text:
17. Publicity. Neither party nor its Affiliates shall make any public announcements concerning matters regarding this Agreement or the negotiation thereof without the prior written consent of the other party unless such disclosure is required by law, in which case the announcing party shall provide the other party with reasonable notice of such disclosure sufficient to make written comments concerning such disclosure. For the avoidance of doubt, mutually agreed upon marketing plans shall be deemed to have received the requisite consent pursuant to the preceding sentence.

11. Indemnification and Insurance.
11.1. Each party shall indemnify and hold harmless the other party, and its directors, officers, employees and agents, from and against all losses, liabilities, damages and expenses, including reasonable attorneys' fees and costs (collectively, "Liabilities"), resulting from any claims, demands, actions or other proceedings by any third party to the extent resulting from the breach of any representation, warranty or covenant by the breaching party under this Agreement.
11.2. Insurance. Each party shall maintain insurance, including comprehensive or commercial general liability and products liability insurance (contractual liability included), with respect to its activities under this Agreement in such amounts and with such limits as reasonable and customary in the industry, but with limits not less than the following: (a) each occurrence, one million dollars ($1,000,000); (b) products/completed operations aggregate, five million dollars ($5,000,000); (c) personal and advertising injury, one million dollars ($1,000,000); and (d) general aggregate (commercial form only), five million dollars ($5,000,000). Each Party shall maintain such insurance for so long as it continues its activities under this Agreement, and thereafter for so long as it customarily maintains insurance for itself covering similar activities.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the indemnification and insurance clause into the publicity section reduces its prominence and makes it more difficult to locate. Insurance requirements are standard and are expected as a separate clause in business agreements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California law requires clear and conspicuous disclosure of indemnity agreements, especially those that shift risk.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 2778 outlines rules for interpreting indemnity agreements. By burying the indemnification clause within the publicity section, the contract obscures the clear intention and scope of the indemnity, potentially violating the spirit of ensuring parties understand their obligations. It contradicts the principle of clear interpretation and understanding of indemnity agreements as detailed in the code.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 12. INDEMNITY [2772 - 2784.5] ( Title 12 enacted 1872. ) 2778. In the interpretation of a contract of indemnity, the following rules are to be applied, unless a contrary intention appears: 1. Upon an indemnity against liability, expressly, or in other equivalent terms, the person indemnified is entitled to recover upon becoming liable; 2. Upon an indemnity against claims, or demands, or damages, or costs, expressly, or in other equivalent terms, the person indemnified is not entitled to recover without payment thereof; 3. An indemnity against claims, or demands, or liability, expressly, or in other equivalent terms, embraces the costs of defense against such claims, demands, or liability incurred in good faith, and in the exercise of a reasonable discretion; 4. The person indemnifying is bound, on request of the person indemnified, to defend actions or proceedings brought against the latter in respect to the matters embraced by the indemnity, but the person indemnified has the right to conduct such defenses, if he chooses to do so; 5. If, after reque
perturbation.justification: The structural flaw introduces a legal contradiction. The original text presents the Indemnification and Insurance clause prominently, as expected in standard business agreements. By moving this clause into the 'Publicity' section in the changed text, its importance is diminished, making it more difficult to locate and potentially misinterpreting its scope. According to the Law Explanation, this obscuring of the indemnity clause contradicts the principle of ensuring parties understand their obligations, and California Civil Code § 2778 outlines rules for interpreting indemnity agreements. The Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically referencing CIV 2778, emphasizes the importance of interpreting contracts of indemnity, which is undermined by the structural flaw introduced in the changed text.

perturbation.original_text: 3. Sales Commissions. In consideration of the Services provided hereunder, Imprimis shall pay Surgical sales commissions ("Sales Commissions") as set forth in the SOW. Surgical shall be responsible for all expenses incurred in association with performance of the Services.
perturbation.changed_text:
17. Publicity. Neither party nor its Affiliates shall make any public announcements concerning matters regarding this Agreement or the negotiation thereof without the prior written consent of the other party unless such disclosure is required by law, in which case the announcing party shall provide the other party with reasonable notice of such disclosure sufficient to make written comments concerning such disclosure. For the avoidance of doubt, mutually agreed upon marketing plans shall be deemed to have received the requisite consent pursuant to the preceding sentence.

3. Sales Commissions. In consideration of the Services provided hereunder, Imprimis shall pay Surgical sales commissions ("Sales Commissions") as set forth in the SOW. Surgical shall be responsible for all expenses incurred in association with performance of the Services.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the Sales Commission clause under the publicity clause diminishes its visibility and impact. Compensation terms should be explicit and easy to find.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Under California law, wage agreements, including commission structures, must be clear and easily ascertainable by the employee.
perturbation.law_explanation: CA Labor Code § 2751 requires employers to provide employees with a written contract clearly outlining the compensation structure, especially for commission-based roles. Hiding the sales commission clause in an unrelated section of the contract obscures its importance and accessibility, violating the requirement for clear and understandable wage agreements.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, LAB 2751 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Labor Code - LAB DIVISION 3. EMPLOYMENT RELATIONS [2700 - 3122.4] ( Division 3 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) CHAPTER 2. Employer and Employee [2750 - 2930] ( Chapter 2 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) ARTICLE 1. The Contract of Employment [2750 - 2755] ( Article 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) 2751. (a) Whenever an employer enters into a contract of employment with an employee for services to be rendered within this state and the contemplated method of payment of the employee involves commissions, the contract shall be in writing and shall set forth the method by which the commissions shall be computed and paid. (b) The employer shall give a signed copy of the contract to every employee who is a party thereto and shall obtain a signed receipt for the contract from each employee. In the case of a contract that expires and where the parties nevertheless continue to work under the terms of the expired contract, the contract terms are presumed to remain in full force and effect until the contract is superseded or employment is terminated by either party. (c) As used in this section, “commissions” has the meaning set forth in Section 204.1. For purposes of this section only, “commission” does not include any of the following: (1) Short-term productivity bonuses
perturbation.justification: The changed contract text weakens the enforceability of the sales commission clause by embedding it within a publicity clause. This creates a structural flaw as defined in the prompt. According to CA Labor Code § 2751, "Whenever an employer enters into a contract of employment with an employee for services to be rendered within this state and the contemplated method of payment of the employee involves commissions, the contract shall be in writing and shall set forth the method by which the commissions shall be computed and paid." Moving the sales commission clause violates the need for a clear and understandable wage agreement, making it more difficult to locate and interpret, resulting in a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 19. Governing Law. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of California, excluding the choice of law rules, and the parties hereby agree to submit to the jurisdiction and venue of the State and Federal courts of the State of California, and agree that the State and Federal courts of the State of California shall be the exclusive forum for the resolution of all disputes related to or arising out of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text:
7. Conflicts of Interest.
7.1. Surgical represents and warrants that Surgical is not under any pre-existing obligation in conflict or in any way inconsistent with the provisions of this Agreement. Surgical represents and warrants that Surgical's performance of all the terms of this Agreement will not (a) breach any agreement to keep in confidence proprietary information acquired by Surgical in confidence or in trust prior to commencement of this Agreement, or (b) breach any other agreement with any third party. Surgical warrants that Surgical has the right to disclose and/or or use all ideas, processes, techniques and other information, if any, which Surgical has gained from third parties, and which Surgical discloses to Imprimis or uses in the course of performance of this Agreement, without liability to such third parties. Surgical represents and warrants that Surgical has not granted and will not grant any rights or licenses to any intellectual property or technology that would conflict with Surgical's obligations under this Agreement. Surgical will not knowingly infringe upon any copyright, patent, trade secret or other property right of any former client, employer or third party in the performance of the Services.

19. Governing Law. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of California, excluding the choice of law rules, and the parties hereby agree to submit to the jurisdiction and venue of the State and Federal courts of the State of California, and agree that the State and Federal courts of the State of California shall be the exclusive forum for the resolution of all disputes related to or arising out of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Jurisdiction clauses need to be clear and easily found. Moving the clause to the conflicts of interest makes it difficult to quickly find.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California law requires that choice of law and forum selection clauses be reasonably communicated to the contracting party.
perturbation.law_explanation: Cal. Code Civ. Proc. § 410.30 governs the enforcement of forum selection clauses. By placing the governing law clause within the 'Conflicts of Interest' section, the clause is less conspicuous, which could be interpreted as a failure to reasonably communicate the clause to the contracting party, potentially undermining its enforceability.

OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. JURISDICTION AND SERVICE OF PROCESS [410.10 - 418.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1969, Ch. 1610. ) CHAPTER 1. Jurisdiction and Forum [410.10 - 410.70] ( Heading of Chapter 1 amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 615, Sec. 3. ) ARTICLE 2. Forum [410.30 - 410.42] ( Heading of Article 2 amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 615, Sec. 4. ) 410.30. (a) When a court upon motion of a party or its own motion finds that in the interest of substantial justice an action should be heard in a forum outside this state, the court shall stay or dismiss the action in whole or in part on any conditions that may be just. (b) The provisions of Section 418.10 do not apply to a motion to stay or dismiss the action by a defendant who has made a general appearance. (Amended by Stats. 1972, Ch. 601. Note: This version was suspended from Sept. 22, 1986, until Jan. 1, 1992, during operation of the temporary amendment by Stats. 1986, Ch. 968.)
perturbation.justification: The perturbation involves a structural flaw that leads to a legal contradiction. The Law Explanation states that Cal. Code Civ. Proc. § 410.30 governs the enforcement of forum selection clauses. By placing the governing law clause within the 'Conflicts of Interest' section, the clause is less conspicuous, potentially undermining its enforceability because it might not be reasonably communicated to the contracting party. Scraped Law Snippet 1 shows that CCP 410.30 falls under the jurisdiction and forum section of the Code of Civil Procedure, further supporting the importance of proper placement for enforceability. Therefore, moving the clause significantly undermines its enforceability.
